serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of garlic oil noodles with steamed pork rib is approximately 380 g, comprising a portion of egg noodles tossed in garlic-infused oil and one or two steamed pork rib pieces with condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles with pork, mixed dish — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (egg noodles cooked, pork ribs, garlic, vegetable oil, soy sauce) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for Southeast Asian noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / FAO INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Garlic Oil Noodles with Steamed Pork Rib exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated by weighted component analysis: cooked egg noodles (~55% by weight, USDA FDC #168930), steamed pork spare ribs (~30% by weight, USDA FDC #167904), garlic oil and condiments (~15% by weight, USDA FDC #169096 for garlic, #04053 for vegetable oil, soy sauce #16124). Macros cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle dish analogues. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and mixed noodle dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Garlic Oil Noodles with Steamed Pork Rib. GI of 47 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles (GI ~46–50, mean 47; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The dish is predominantly egg noodles by weight; fat and protein from pork rib and garlic oil further attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 74.1) / 100 ≈ 35.3 g (serving carbohydrate: 19.5 g/100g × 380 g serving = 74.1 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 380 g serving provides approximately 1596 mg sodium (67% of the 2400 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board), making this dish a significant sodium load for individuals managing hypertension; portion reduction and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from egg noodle carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low GI of approximately 47, the large noodle portion delivers a glycemic load of approximately 35 g per serving, which can produce a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of noodles, increase vegetable accompaniments, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork rib and elevated sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contributes approximately 10.6 g saturated fat and 1596 mg sodium per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, request leaner pork cuts, and choose reduced-sodium soy sauce to reduce atherogenic and hypertensive risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Oil Noodles with Steamed Pork Rib is a Thai-influenced noodle dish served in Singapore's casual-dining and restaurant scene. The dish consists of cooked egg noodles tossed in fragrant garlic-infused oil, typically seasoned with light soy sauce, oyster sauce, and a drizzle of sesame oil, then topped with one or two pieces of tender steamed pork spare rib. The noodles are silky and carry a pronounced roasted-garlic aroma, while the pork rib is soft, mildly sweet, and savoury from the steaming marinade. Garnishes commonly include sliced spring onion and fried shallots. Nutritionally, the dish is moderately energy-dense at approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with a balanced macro profile of roughly 10.8 g protein, 9.2 g fat, and 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The primary clinical concern is sodium: soy sauce and oyster sauce contribute approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g, translating to over 1500 mg per standard restaurant serving. The glycemic index is estimated at 47 (low), anchored to egg noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 35 g due to the large noodle portion. The dish is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets. It is relevant for dietary counselling in hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management contexts.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Garlic Oil Noodles with Steamed Pork Rib was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked egg noodles, steamed pork spare ribs, garlic, vegetable oil, and soy sauce, cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle dish analogues. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database egg noodle entry. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the component sources used (USDA FDC and FAO/INFOODS SEA); pork rib contains negligible vitamin D and the value is not reported in the primary source entries. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated component-derived values where defensible data existed. Sodium is a primary clinical concern at approximately 420 mg/100 g. The cuisine tag in the input record lists 'Thai' as the primary cuisine; the dish is classified as Thai for multilingual naming purposes, requiring a Thai-script local name. The input cuisine_tags also include 'Salad', 'BBQ', and 'Fried Rice Vermicelli', which are restaurant menu category tags from field survey and do not reflect the dish's actual classification. GI estimated from egg noodle analogue (University of Sydney GI Database, GI ~47); no direct measurement exists for this specific dish. GL per serving = (47 × 74.1) / 100 ≈ 35.3 g based on 380 g serving size.
